title: The Jade That Chose Him
content:
The tomb wasn't supposed to be here.

Wei Chen pressed his palm flat against the cold stone wall, feeling for the draft he'd noticed three paces back. His fingers found it—a hairline crack, barely wide enough for a snake to pass. But air moved through it, and where air moved, there was space beyond.

"Boss, we've been at this for six hours." Liu Hao's voice echoed from behind, carrying the whine of a man who'd never learned patience. "This mountain's picked clean. Every tomb within a hundred li has been cracked open since the Song Dynasty. There's nothing left."

Wei Chen didn't turn around. "Then explain the wall."

"Explain what? It's a wall. It's rocks. You've got rocks on the brain."

He smiled, though Liu couldn't see it. That was the difference between a grave robber and a tomb raider. The robber saw rocks. The raider saw what the rocks were hiding.

Wei Chen had been doing this for eighteen years, since he was twelve years old and small enough to squeeze through the gaps his father couldn't fit. He'd crawled through the bowels of dynasties, through the burial chambers of kings and the secret vaults of warlords. He'd held jade that had last seen sunlight when the Han Empire ruled the Central Plains. He'd read inscriptions that would have gotten a scholar executed for treason.

And he'd learned one truth that mattered above all others: the best tombs were never the ones anyone knew about.

"The structure shifts here," he said, tracing the wall's surface. "See how the mortar changes color? This section was sealed later. Much later. Someone didn't want anyone finding what's behind it."

Liu Hao shuffled closer, his lamp casting dancing shadows across the carved stone. He was a big man, built like a brick outhouse, useful for carrying tools and not much else. "So we break through?"

"No. We find the door."

Wei Chen worked his way along the wall, counting his steps. Seven paces east, then a turn. His fingers brushed over carved characters—funerary inscriptions, prayers for the dead, warnings for the living. Standard fare. But beneath his touch, the stone felt wrong. Too smooth in some places, too rough in others.

He'd learned to read stone the way a musician read notes. Every chisel mark told a story. Every patch of discoloration whispered secrets.

There. His hand stopped at a section that looked identical to the rest, but felt different. Warmer. As if the stone had been breathing.

"Bring me the chisel," he said.

"You're going to break it anyway? I thought you said—"

"The chisel, Liu."

The big man grumbled but fetched the tool from their pack. Wei Chen took it, positioned its tip at a seam that was invisible to the eye but obvious to his fingers, and struck.

The stone didn't crack.

It *sighed*.

A sound like an old man waking from a long sleep. The wall split along a line that hadn't been there a moment ago, and beyond it, darkness poured out. Not the ordinary darkness of a tomb, but something thicker. Older. It had weight.

Wei Chen raised his lamp.

The passage beyond was narrow, barely wide enough for one man. The walls were smooth as polished glass, and they didn't look like any stone he'd ever seen. Black, with veins of deep green running through it like rivers through a night sky.

"What is this place?" Liu Hao whispered.

Wei Chen didn't answer. He stepped forward.

The passage curved gently, spiraling downward. Wei Chen counted his steps again—one hundred and eight, a number that meant something in the old texts. One hundred and eight earthly desires. One hundred and eight beads on a monk's rosary. One hundred and eight steps into the unknown.

The passage opened into a chamber.

And Wei Chen stopped breathing.

The room was circular, perhaps thirty paces across, its ceiling lost in shadows above. The walls were covered in murals, but these weren't the typical tomb paintings of servants and concubines, of processions and paradise. These showed something else entirely.

Celestial beings. Hundreds of them. Thousands. Arrayed in battle formation, their weapons blazing with light. And above them all, on a throne that seemed to float among clouds, a figure in golden robes. His face was serene, his eyes half-closed, and in his hands he held a jade seal.

The Jade Emperor.

Wei Chen had seen depictions of the Jade Emperor before—every temple in China had them. But never like this. Never with such detail, such *life*. The paint seemed to move in the corner of his vision, the celestial soldiers shifting their stances, the Jade Emperor's fingers twitching on his seal.

"This is a thousand-year-old tomb," Liu Hao said, his voice hollow. "These paintings should be dust. How are they still..."

"Because they're not paintings," Wei Chen said.

He walked to the center of the chamber, where a pedestal rose from the floor. On it sat a piece of jade the size of his fist. It was green, but not any green he'd ever seen. It was the green of spring leaves after rain, of jadeite from the deepest Burmese mines, of something that existed more as an idea than a physical object.

It glowed.

Not with reflected light from his lamp. The glow came from within, a soft luminescence that pulsed like a heartbeat.

"The hell is that?" Liu Hao took a step back. "Boss, I don't like this. This place is wrong."

Wei Chen reached for the jade.

"Boss, don't—"

His fingers touched the surface.

And the world exploded.

---

The pain was indescribable.

Not physical. Wei Chen had broken bones before, had taken cuts that needed thirty stitches, had been stabbed in a fight over a Tang dynasty vase. This was none of those things. This was his *soul* being grabbed by something infinite and ancient, being pulled apart and examined, every memory, every thought, every hidden corner of his being laid bare.

He tried to scream, but he had no mouth. He tried to run, but he had no body. He was consciousness without form, floating in an ocean of light.

And then, a voice.

Not spoken. Not heard. Simply *known*.

[Heavenly Court Status: Collapsed]
[Throne of the Jade Emperor: Vacant]
[Duration of Vacancy: 10,427 years, 6 months, 3 days, 14 hours, 22 minutes]

[Searching for compatible host...]
[Compatibility: 0.03%]
[Warning: Host is mundane. No cultivation base. No spiritual affinity.]

[Alternative candidates detected: None within range.]
[Proceeding with binding.]

[Binding in progress...]

Wei Chen's consciousness screamed.

[Binding at 12%... 34%... 67%...]

The light around him changed, coalescing into shapes. He saw a palace made of clouds, its pillars carved from jade and gold. He saw gardens where immortal peaches grew, their fruit radiating power. He saw armies of celestial soldiers standing guard, their armor gleaming, their spears sharp.

And he saw them fall.

A darkness swept across the Heavenly Court like a tide of ink. The celestials fought, their techniques shaking the foundations of reality itself. But the darkness consumed them, one by one, their screams echoing across dimensions.

The Jade Emperor sat on his throne, watching. His face was calm, but his eyes held infinite sadness. He raised his jade seal, and the Heavenly Court began to change. Walls grew thicker. Corridors twisted. The palace became something else entirely.

A dungeon.

[Binding at 89%... 94%...]

The darkness reached the throne room. The Jade Emperor stood, his golden robes flowing, his seal glowing with the light of a thousand suns.

"You are not the first," he said, and his voice carried across the void. "You will not be the last. But you are the one who found me. The one who touched the core. The one who will rebuild what was lost."

He smiled, and it was the saddest smile Wei Chen had ever seen.

"Good luck, little tomb raider. You'll need it."

[Binding complete.]

[Welcome, Dungeon Core Host Wei Chen.]

[Heavenly Palace Dungeon: Floor 1 Activated.]

[Current Resources: 0 Qi]
[Current Buildable Rooms: 0]
[Current Summonable Guardians: 0]
[Current Floor Level: 1]

[Warning: Dungeon is critically damaged. Immediate reconstruction required.]
[Warning: Dark forces sense the return of the Heavenly Court. Estimated time before detection: 72 hours.]
[Warning: Dungeon is attracting ambient Qi. Local cultivators will investigate.]

[Quest: Survive the First Trial]
[Objective: Construct Floor 1 of the Heavenly Palace Dungeon]
[Time Limit: 72 hours]
[Reward: Unlock Floor 2, Summon First Guardian]
[Penalty for Failure: Dungeon Core destroyed. Host soul annihilated.]

Wei Chen opened his eyes.

He was still in the chamber, but everything had changed. The murals on the walls were gone, replaced by rough stone. The pedestal was cracked, the jade gone. He looked down at his hands and saw lines of green light pulsing beneath his skin, tracing paths through his veins like rivers on a map.

"Boss?" Liu Hao's voice came from somewhere distant. "Boss, what happened? Your eyes—they're green. They're *glowing* green."

Wei Chen tried to speak, but his voice came out wrong. Too deep. Too resonant. As if he was speaking from a hundred mouths at once.

"I'm fine," he said, which was a lie. "I'm... changed."

"You touched that jade and you started screaming and then you—you *disappeared*. For like five minutes. And then you came back and your eyes were glowing and I think I need to get the hell out of here."

Liu Hao was backing toward the passage, his face white, his hands shaking.

"Wait," Wei Chen said. "Don't—"

But Liu Hao was already gone, his footsteps echoing as he fled up the spiral passage.

Wei Chen stood alone in the chamber, the weight of the dungeon pressing against his consciousness. He could feel it now—the shape of the Heavenly Palace above him, its thousand floors collapsed into rubble, its corridors twisted and broken. He could feel the darkness pressing against its boundaries, ancient and hungry, waiting for the moment when the dungeon's defenses failed.

And he could feel something else. A presence. Sleeping, but not dead. Waiting.

The Jade Emperor's voice echoed in his memory: *You'll need it.*

The system message flickered in his vision.

[72 hours remaining.]

Wei Chen looked at his hands again. The green light was fading, sinking back beneath his skin, but he could still feel it there. The dungeon core. The heart of the Heavenly Palace. Bound to his soul.

He was a tomb raider. A grave robber. A man who had spent his life stealing from the dead.

And now the dead had made him their heir.

Above him, through the broken structure of the dungeon, he sensed movement. Cultivators, drawn by the sudden surge of Qi. Dozens of them, at least, approaching from all directions. Some curious. Some greedy. Some with weapons drawn.

They would reach the dungeon entrance in hours.

They would find a floor that was nothing but rubble and shadows.

They would tear it apart looking for treasures.

And when they found the core—when they found *him*—

Wei Chen closed his eyes and felt the dungeon respond to his will. Stone shifted.

title: The First Floor of Heaven
content:
Wei Chen’s eyes snapped open.

Cold stone pressed against his back. The air smelled of dust and something metallic—old blood, perhaps, or rust. Above him, a ceiling of carved jade stretched into darkness, its surface etched with constellations that pulsed with a faint, dying light.

He sat up too fast. His head spun, and his hand went instinctively to his chest. The jade shard was still there, lodged beneath his sternum like a second heart. He could feel it now—not just physically, but *conceptually*. It hummed in his awareness, a constant low vibration that seemed to synchronize with his pulse.

*The Heavenly Dungeon Core*, it had called itself. *The throne of the Jade Emperor.*

“Right,” Wei Chen muttered, his voice echoing in the empty chamber. “Because my life wasn’t complicated enough.”

He stood, brushing dust from his robes—the same coarse fabric he’d worn into the tomb, now torn and stained. He’d been a tomb raider for fifteen years. He’d broken into cursed burial mounds, dodged ghost-traps, and outrun corpse kings. But never—*never*—had he woken up with a piece of celestial jade fused to his ribcage.

The chamber around him was vast. Pillars of white marble rose toward the unseen ceiling, each one carved with dragons mid-flight. Their eyes were hollow sockets now, but Wei Chen could feel the *intent* behind them—the lingering echo of a power that had once made the heavens tremble.

*This is the first floor of the Heavenly Palace,* the core’s voice whispered in his mind. *The Hall of Azure Clouds. Once, it welcomed visiting immortals. Now, it welcomes you.*

“I’m not an immortal,” Wei Chen said flatly. “I’m a grave robber who made a very bad decision.”

*You are the Dungeon Lord. The decision was not bad—it was inevitable.*

Wei Chen grimaced. “I hate prophecies.”

He walked forward, his footsteps echoing in the silence. The hall stretched on, impossibly long, as if space itself had been folded. At the far end, a throne sat upon a raised dais—simple, unadorned, but *heavy* with presence. It was carved from the same jade as the shard in his chest, and as he approached, he felt the core pulse in response.

*Sit,* the core urged. *Claim your seat.*

“I’m not sitting in a dead god’s chair.”

*The Jade Emperor is not dead. He is… absent. And his throne awaits a new occupant.*

Wei Chen stopped at the base of the dais. The throne was close enough to touch, but he held back. Something about it felt *wrong*—or perhaps *right* in a way that terrified him. He’d spent his life avoiding attention, slipping through cracks in the world. The last thing he wanted was to sit on a throne.

“What happened to the old emperor?” he asked instead.

Silence. The core’s presence flickered, as if it were accessing memories buried deep.

*The Heavenly Court fell ten thousand years ago. The dark forces that destroyed it are not gone—they are merely sleeping. Waiting. And now that the core has awakened, they will sense it.*

Wei Chen’s blood ran cold. “Let me guess. They’ll come for me.”

*They will come for the throne. You are merely the one sitting on it.*

“I’m not sitting on it yet.”

*You are bound to it. The distinction is academic.*

Wei Chen closed his eyes and took a long breath. He’d survived worse odds. Probably. He chose not to think too hard about it.

“Fine,” he said. “If I’m stuck with this thing, I need to know what I’m working with. Give me the tour.”

The core’s presence pulsed, and the hall shifted. The pillars of jade shimmered, and suddenly Wei Chen could *see*—not with his eyes, but with his mind. The dungeon unfolded before him like a map painted on his consciousness.

**Heavenly Dungeon – Floor 1: Hall of Azure Clouds**
- **Status:** Damaged (67% structural integrity)
- **Defenses:** None (guardian seals broken)
- **Resource Generation:** Negligible (celestial qi leak detected)
- **Threat Level:** Low (no active enemies detected)

“Sixty-seven percent?” Wei Chen said. “That’s generous.”

*The hall has suffered greatly. The celestial qi that once sustained it has bled into the mortal realm. You must repair the seals and restore the flow.*

“And how do I do that?”

*Cultivators. They carry qi within them. When they enter the dungeon, their energy feeds the core. The stronger they are, the more they provide.*

Wei Chen’s eyes narrowed. “You want me to lure cultivators here. Like bait.”

*Not bait. Pilgrims. The Heavenly Palace was once a place of enlightenment. Cultivators came to test themselves, to gain insights, to advance. In return, their qi sustained the palace. It was a symbiotic relationship.*

“And now?”

*Now, they will come to conquer. The dungeon has become a trial—a place of danger and reward. Those who survive will grow stronger. Those who fall will feed the core.*

Wei Chen rubbed his temples. “So I’m building a death trap for power-hungry cultivators.”

*You are rebuilding Heaven. One floor at a time.*

There was a pause. The core’s presence flickered again, and Wei Chen felt a sudden spike of alarm.

*Something approaches.*

“What? Where?”

*The outer gate. A cultivator. He is weak—barely at the Qi Condensation stage. But he carries a talisman of detection. He will find the entrance soon.*

Wei Chen’s heart hammered. He was a tomb raider, not a fighter. His skills lay in stealth and cunning, not direct combat. If a cultivator found him now…

*You are the Dungeon Lord. You do not fight—you command. The hall has no guardians, but it has traps. Ancient ones. I can activate them, but they require qi to function.*

“How much qi?”

*The amount you possess. Which is none.*

“Great.”

*But you have the shard. It holds a reserve—enough for one activation. Choose wisely.*

Wei Chen looked around the hall. The pillars, the throne, the empty sockets of the dragon carvings. This was his domain now, broken and bleeding as it was. If he wanted to survive, he needed to start acting like a lord.

“Activate the traps,” he said. “All of them.”

*The reserves will be depleted. You will have no energy for anything else.*

“If that cultivator gets in here and finds me helpless, I won’t need energy. I’ll need a coffin.”

The core pulsed—was that amusement?—and the hall groaned. The jade constellations on the ceiling flickered, and Wei Chen felt a surge of power flow from the shard into the stone beneath his feet. The dragon carvings’ eyes glowed red.

**Trap Matrix: Azure Clouds Formation – Activated**
- **Trap 1:** Mist of Confusion (disorients intruders)
- **Trap 2:** Jade Serpent Strike (projectile attack)
- **Trap 3:** Gravity Well (slows movement)
- **Duration:** 1 hour (qi reserves critical)

Wei Chen felt the weight of the activation settle on him like a physical burden. His limbs grew heavy, and his vision swam for a moment. The shard in his chest throbbed, but it was warm now—alive.

“He’s coming,” the core said aloud, its voice echoing through the hall. “The outer gate has opened.”

Wei Chen moved. He couldn’t fight, but he could observe. He climbed the dais and positioned himself behind the throne, peering through a gap in the jade. The hall stretched before him, empty and silent, waiting.

Minutes passed. Then—

The doors at the far end of the hall creaked open.

A figure stepped through. He was young, maybe early twenties, with the lean build of a martial artist. He wore blue robes embroidered with silver clouds—the insignia of the Azure Cloud Sect, a minor cultivation sect from the eastern provinces. In his hand, he held a jade compass, its needle spinning wildly.

“By the ancestors,” the young cultivator breathed, his eyes wide as he took in the hall. “It’s real. The legends were *real*.”

He stepped forward cautiously, his free hand resting on the hilt of his sword. His qi was faint, barely a flicker—Qi Condensation stage, as the core had said. A mortal who had just begun to cultivate.

Wei Chen felt a pang of sympathy. The boy had no idea what he was walking into.

The cultivator took another step. Then another. The Mist of Confusion began to seep from the pillars—thin, silvery wisps that curled around his ankles. He didn’t notice at first. He was too entranced by the carvings, the throne, the sheer *presence* of the hall.

“The Heavenly Palace,” he whispered. “If I can claim a treasure here, I’ll be promoted to Inner Disciple. I’ll finally be someone.”

*Poor fool,* the core whispered in Wei Chen’s mind. *He seeks glory in a grave.*

The mist thickened. The cultivator stopped, blinking, his hand going to his head. “What… what is this?”

The Mist of Confusion took hold. The boy’s eyes glazed over, and he staggered, his compass clattering to the floor. He turned in a slow circle, lost, his sword drawn but useless.

“Where… where am I?”

Wei Chen watched from behind the throne. The traps were working. The boy was helpless. All he had to do was wait for the mist to drain his qi, and he would collapse. Then Wei Chen could decide what to do with him.

But as he watched the young cultivator stumble, lost and terrified, something twisted in Wei Chen’s chest. He’d been that boy once—desperate, hungry for power, willing to risk everything for a chance to rise above his station.

*No,* he told himself. *This is survival. If I let him go, he’ll bring more. The sects will come. The dark forces will come. I can’t afford mercy.*

The cultivator fell to his knees, gasping. His sword slipped from his grasp.

“Please,” he whispered, his voice breaking. “Anyone… help me…”

Wei Chen closed his eyes.

And then the hall exploded.

Stone shattered. Light blazed. Wei Chen was thrown from his hiding spot, slamming into the base of the dais. The Mist of Confusion scattered, torn apart by a wave of golden energy that radiated from the entrance.

A figure stood in the doorway. Tall. Armored in bronze and jade. His face was hidden behind a mask of a snarling lion, and in his hand, he held a spear that crackled with lightning.

“So,” the figure said, his voice deep and resonant, “the Heavenly Dungeon awakens. And a tomb raider sits on the throne.”

Wei Chen’s blood ran cold. The newcomer’s qi was immense—a pressure that made the air itself feel heavy. This was no Qi Condensation novice. This was a Core Formation elder, or worse.

The young cultivator looked up, relief flooding his face. “Senior

title: The First Floor Takes Shape
content:
The golden light faded from Wei Chen's eyes, leaving him blinking in the sudden dimness of the throne room. The Heavenly Dungeon Core pulsed beneath his palm, warm as a living heart, and he could feel it now—really feel it—like an extension of his own flesh.

He pulled his hand away and stared at the jade pillar. Cracks still spiderwebbed its surface, but the light no longer leaked from them. Instead, the glow had retreated inward, concentrating in the core like a dying ember waiting for fuel.

"So," he muttered, wiping sweat from his brow, "I'm bonded to a broken god-palace. Fantastic."

A ripple of energy answered him. Not words—the core didn't speak in human language—but impressions. Hunger. Need. Potential.

Wei Chen closed his eyes and focused on the connection. The dungeon spread before him like a blueprint burned into his skull. One floor. Just one. The Celestial Audience Hall, where the Jade Emperor once received tributes from lesser gods and immortals.

The room where he currently stood.

But the blueprint showed more. Empty chambers branching off the main hall. Corridors that led nowhere. Staircases that descended into darkness, waiting for him to build the next floor. And beneath it all, the foundation—a massive formation array that had once drawn qi from the very fabric of heaven.

Now it drew from him.

Wei Chen's knees buckled as he felt the drain. Not spiritual energy—he was a mortal, he barely had any qi—but something else. Life force. Vitality. The dungeon was feeding on his existence to sustain itself.

"Not happening," he growled, forcing himself upright. "I need fuel. Real fuel."

He reached into his robe and pulled out the only valuable thing he carried: a jade talisman he'd stolen from a Ming dynasty tomb three years ago. The thing was supposed to ward off evil spirits. He'd never tested it, but the antique dealers in Beijing had offered him fifty thousand yuan.

Wei Chen pressed the talisman against the core.

The jade pillar drank it in. The talisman crumbled to dust in his hands, its spiritual essence sucked dry in less than a heartbeat. But the core pulsed brighter, and Wei Chen felt something shift in the dungeon's layout.

A new chamber had opened. A meditation room, according to the blueprint. Empty, but ready.

"Okay," he said, his voice steadier now. "I understand. Feed you treasures, get rooms. Feed you better treasures, get better rooms. Like a gacha game, except the price is my life."

The core hummed agreement.

Wei Chen spent the next hour exploring. The Celestial Audience Hall was massive—easily the size of a football field, with golden pillars rising into mist-shrouded heights. The floor was polished white jade, veined with gold, and the walls were covered in murals depicting the old gods: the Four Heavenly Kings, the Eight Immortals, the Dragon Kings of the Four Seas.

All empty now. The murals were just paintings. The thrones along the walls were just furniture.

But the potential...

Wei Chen found the first side chamber. It was smaller, maybe twenty meters square, with a single altar in the center. Dust covered everything. The air was stale, tasting of ozone and old incense.

"First floor, first room," he said, running his fingers along the altar. "What do I put here? Traps? Monsters? A sign that says 'Welcome to Heaven, please take a number'?"

The core pulsed. Images flooded his mind.

*Guardians. Traps. Trials. Tests of worth.*

Right. This was a dungeon. Cultivators would come seeking treasures, seeking breakthroughs, seeking the favor of the Jade Emperor. They would fight through these rooms, overcome challenges, and if they proved worthy, they would receive rewards.

If they proved unworthy, they would die.

Wei Chen felt the weight of that responsibility settle on his shoulders. He was no god. He was a tomb raider who'd stumbled into something far beyond his understanding. But the core had chosen him, and the core didn't make mistakes.

*It chose me because I was there,* he thought bitterly. *Because I was stupid enough to touch it.*

No. That wasn't right. The core had been waiting. For ten thousand years, it had waited for someone to find it, to bond with it, to become its new master. And Wei Chen, with his dirty hands and empty pockets and desperate heart, had been the one to answer.

"Fine," he said. "Let's see what I'm working with."

He focused on the core, reaching for its knowledge. The dungeon had a repository of memories—fragments of the old Heavenly Court, echoes of the gods who had once walked these halls. Wei Chen sifted through them, looking for something useful.

He found the inventory.

It was pitiful. A few broken artifacts. Some scattered spirit stones, their qi long depleted. A single jade slip containing a basic cultivation technique—the kind given to outer disciples of minor sects.

But there was something else. Something buried deep in the core's memory, locked behind layers of spiritual encryption.

A name.

*Zhao Tianming.*

Wei Chen pulled back, startled. The name burned in his mind like a brand. He didn't know who Zhao Tianming was, but the core's reaction told him everything he needed to know.

Fear. The core feared this name.

And then came the vision.

The Heavenly Court in flames. Golden pillars crashing down. Celestial soldiers screaming as shadowy figures tore through them like paper. And at the center of it all, a man in black robes, his face hidden behind a mask of living darkness, his hands dripping with the blood of gods.

Zhao Tianming.

The one who had destroyed the old Heavenly Court.

The vision faded, leaving Wei Chen gasping on the floor. His hands were shaking. His heart pounded against his ribs like a caged animal.

"That was... that was real, wasn't it?" he whispered. "That's what happened. That's why the court fell."

The core pulsed. Sadness. Grief. And a desperate, burning need.

*He will return.*

The words formed in Wei Chen's mind, cold as winter steel. *He will return, and he will finish what he started. Unless you stop him.*

Wei Chen laughed. It was a hollow sound, echoing through the empty hall. "Me? Stop a guy who killed gods? I can't even afford a proper meal. I'm sleeping in abandoned temples and stealing from tombs just to survive."

But even as he said it, he felt the core's determination flowing into him. It had chosen him for a reason. Not because he was strong, or wise, or worthy. Because he was desperate. Because he had nothing left to lose.

And desperate men could do terrible things.

"Alright," Wei Chen said, pushing himself to his feet. "Let's start small. First floor, first room. What do I need?"

The core answered with another flood of information.

*Qi concentration. Formation arrays. Guardian summoning. Trap mechanisms. Reward distribution.*

It was overwhelming. Like trying to drink the ocean through a straw. But Wei Chen forced himself to focus, breaking it down into manageable pieces.

Step one: Gather qi. The dungeon was starved, running on fumes and his own life force. He needed to feed it spirit stones, treasures, anything with spiritual energy. The more he fed it, the more it could do.

Step two: Build the formation. The foundation array was intact, but it needed to be activated and calibrated. That would require precise placement of spirit stones and the inscription of countless talismanic characters.

Step three: Summon guardians. The dungeon could create spiritual constructs—echoes of the old Heavenly Army—but only if it had enough energy. And those constructs would need to be bound to specific rooms, given specific instructions.

Step four: Set the trials. Cultivators wouldn't just walk through an empty dungeon. They needed challenges. Tests. Rewards. The whole thing had to be balanced, difficult enough to be interesting but not so hard that everyone died.

Wei Chen rubbed his temples. "This is going to take forever."

The core pulsed. *You have three days.*

"What?"

*Three days. Then the first cultivators will arrive. The dungeon's awakening has already sent ripples through the mortal realm. They will come seeking answers. Seeking power.*

Wei Chen's blood ran cold. "Three days? I don't even have a single trap set up. I don't have any guardians. I don't have—"

*You have me,* the core interrupted. *And you have yourself. That will be enough.*

"Will it?"

The core didn't answer. But Wei Chen felt its presence settle around him like a cloak, warm and reassuring. He was not alone. The dungeon was with him. The old Heavenly Court, broken and battered, had placed its trust in him.

He couldn't let it down.

"Alright," he said, rolling up his sleeves. "Let's get to work."

The next three days were a blur of activity. Wei Chen slept in snatches, ate when he remembered, and spent every waking moment working on the dungeon. He fed the core everything he had—the jade talisman, a bronze mirror he'd stolen from a Tang dynasty tomb, a handful of copper coins that had once been used in a Song dynasty temple.

Each offering made the core pulse brighter, and each pulse unlocked new knowledge. He learned how to inscribe formation arrays, how to channel qi through the dungeon's veins, how to create spiritual constructs from nothing but will and energy.

By the end of the second day, the first floor had begun to take shape.

The Celestial Audience Hall was no longer empty. Golden light now pulsed along the walls, illuminating the murals with a soft, ethereal glow. The air was thick with qi, rich and nourishing, and Wei Chen felt his own body strengthening as he breathed it in.

The meditation room was finished. He'd placed a simple cushion on the floor, inscribed with a formation that would help cultivators focus their minds and refine their qi. It wasn't much, but it was a start.

And he'd created his first guardian.

It stood in the corner of the audience hall, a construct of golden light and spiritual energy. It looked like a celestial soldier—armor gleaming, spear held at attention—but its face was blank, featureless. A placeholder, waiting for a soul.

Wei Chen had named it "Guardian One." He wasn't very creative.

"This is pathetic," he muttered, staring at the construct. "One guard. One room. And I'm pretty sure a Qi Condensation cultivator could punch right through it."

The core pulsed. *It will grow. As you grow. As the dungeon grows.*

"I don't have time to grow. Cultivators are coming tomorrow."

*Then we must prepare.*

Wei Chen sighed. "Any bright ideas?"

The core was silent for a long moment. Then it showed him something new. A memory, buried deep in its archives. A technique used by the old Heavenly Court—a way to create illusions so real they could kill.

"Now we're talking," Wei Chen said, a grin spreading across his face.

He spent the rest of the second day and all of the third learning the technique. It was difficult—far more difficult than anything he'd attempted before—but the core guided him, feeding him the precise patterns of qi, the exact sequences of mental focus.

By the evening of the third day, he had it.

The audience hall now had a second layer. To the naked eye, it looked the same—golden pillars, jade floor, misty heights. But beneath the surface, a web of illusionary formations waited. A cultivator who entered without permission
